mnemonics.title: NTP Stratum Levels
mnemonics.content: Remember: Stratums 0,1 are 'Primary Gold' (GPS atomic), Stratum 2-15 get gradually less accurate, Stratum 16 is 'Not Synchronized' (NS). Think: 0-1-2-15-16 = Oops, Free To Sleep (but 16 is No Sync!)

mnemonics.title: Cristian's Algorithm Steps
mnemonics.content: C-S-T-A: Client Sends Request → Server Returns Time → Add RoundTrip/2 → Adjust Clock

mnemonics.title: Berkeley Algorithm Flow
mnemonics.content: M-A-C-A: Master polls All → Compute Average → Send Adjustments to All

acronyms.usage: Standard global time reference for synchronization
Internet protocol for clock synchronization across networks
Satellite system providing accurate time signals used as reference clocks
Total time for message to travel to destination and back

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline showing: Client sends request at T1 → Server receives at T2 → Server sends at T3 (with timestamp) → Client receives at T4. Calculate: Time = ServerTime + (T4-T1)/2 - T2
Central master node with arrows to all slaves showing request/response cycle, then adjustments flowing back to all slaves with delta values.
Pyramid diagram: Top = Stratum 0 (atomic clocks), Level 1 = Primary servers, Level 2 = Secondary servers, etc. Arrows showing synchronization flow downward.
Two parallel timeline lines, one straight (perfect clock), one slanted (drifting clock). The gap between them widens over time representing accumulated error.

analogies.analogy: Like calling a friend for the time - you ask, they tell you their time, you assume it took equal time for your question to reach them and their answer to get back to you.
Like a team leader collecting everyone's watch times, finding the average, and telling everyone how much to adjust their watches.
Like different runners starting at the same time but running at slightly different speeds - over time, they become out of sync.
Like a relay race - Stratum 0 has the original time (starter), passes to Stratum 1 (first runner), then Stratum 2 and so on, with each pass potentially adding small errors.
